# Individual Journal

# avila

**Name:** Clarissa Avila

**Role:** Backend (Register and Login) Creator and Documentation Lead

My main task is on the server-side where I developed the backend API of our system’s user authentication module. I am responsible for the logic user registration and login validation. I also helped in the documentation and divided the tasks and assigned roles to the group members.

        	First, I developed the authentication system using PHP and MySQL. I created a single endpoint for both user registration and login within a single file. I handled incoming requests using php://input and $POST. I used an action parameter to decide if the request id for registration or the login. The two logics now exist in one centralized endpoint.

For registration, I made the input validation that ensures that all the required fields are filled. I also added checks to prevent duplicate usernames and email addresses by checking the database first. Passwords are securely stored using hashing instead of plain-text. If everything is valid, the user data is saved to the database and a JSON response is returned with the correct HTTP status code. For the login, I checked if the email exists in the database and verified the password using password\_verify(). If the login is successful, user detail is returned in a structured JSON response. I ensured that all responses follow a structured JSON format with status, code, message. I also made sure to properly show an error  in JSON format when the database connection fails, so it is clear when the system cannot connect to the database. 

        	However, I encountered several challenges during development. One of the main issues was combining the separate PHP files for authentication and seller  functions into one file where there was an issue in variables and not being able to connect the two files at first. I also experienced common mistakes like missing commas, incorrect operators, typos, and mismatch variables. I also often forget to set HTTP response codes in some parts of the code. Since the API is centralized, a single and small error in one section often affected the other parts.

        	To solve these issues, I improved the routing by properly organizing the action parameter so that authentication runs first, while other functions are handled separately using switch structure. This allowed both authentication and seller functionalities to work together without conflict. I also carefully checked variable names throughout the code to make them consistent. Lastly, I always make sure to validate HTTP response codes for every response to that debugging and the responses are clear.

 

 

# bataller

**Name:** Kristine Mae B. Bataller  
**Role:** Backend (Seller) Creator and Documentation Lead

	As assigned on the server-side, my task is to develop a REST API for Artistryx’s seller functionality. Following the Create, Read, and Update in the CRUD operations, the system logic I implemented includes registering and updating the shop, as well as viewing shop information. First, I created and inserted a table named “sellers” into the existing database, with the necessary attributes. Then I made sure that it is connected to the “users” table to ensure data integrity and proper ownership of each shop.  
After the user successfully logs in, the client-side will present four choices, specifically Register Shop, View Shop, Update Shop, and Logout, which sends corresponding requests to the REST API to process. The register shop uses a POST request that accepts shopName as parameter and first validates the userId. The code also confirms whether the user already has a shop and whether the shop name does not yet exist in the database before accepting the registration and storing it in the database with a default status of “Pending”. Furthermore, View Shop is a GET request that allows the users to see and retrieve the shop details linked to their account. The update shop, on the other hand, is a PUT request that checks if the new name is the same as the current one and checks if it is already taken by another user before proceeding to update the shop name. Aside from coding the server-side seller functionality, I am also in charge of writing the documentation report for our group.  
However, there were few challenges I encountered in database setup. Since I was using the older MySQL version, importing artistryx\_db.sql resulted in error and incompatible issues such as the unrecognized collation utf8mb4\_0900\_ai\_ci. Also, there was confusion in creating the “sellers” table as at first I tried to query first the “sellers” table before the “users”, which was wrong to do.  
In solving these challenges, I changed the collation utf8mb4\_0900\_ai\_ci to utf8mb4\_general\_ci, which is compatible with the older MySQL version in XAMPP. I resolved the second issue by manually running the SQL statements (phyMyAdmin SQL tab) in the correct order.

# capitulo

**Name:** Rein Allen M. Capitulo   
**Role:** Quality Assurance (QA) / Tester  
**Focus:** Responsible for verifying API endpoints, database integrity, and cross-platform communication between the Python client and PHP backend.

**Phase I: Infrastructure Stabilization and Environment Debugging**  
In my capacity as the Tester for the Artistryx platform, my primary objective was to architect a stable local testing environment and ensure the seamless integration of the system’s multi-language components. The project’s initial phase was characterized by significant infrastructure blockers. Upon deploying the Apache and MySQL services via XAMPP, I immediately identified a critical Port 3306 conflict (System Error 10048), which effectively paralyzed the database initialization.

Rather than simply attempting a restart, I utilized advanced terminal diagnostics to perform a process-level investigation. By identifying the specific Process ID (PID) that was erroneously binding to the MySQL port, I was able to clear the "socket hang" and stabilize the server. This intervention was vital, as it prevented a "False Negative" testing scenario where the backend code might have been blamed for what was ultimately an environmental configuration error.

**Phase II: Data Layer Resolution and Schema Refactoring**  
Following the stabilization of the server, my focus shifted to Database Provisioning and Data Layer Resolution. The laboratory faced a major hurdle when the provided artistryx\_db.sql schema failed to import due to a versioning mismatch. The source file utilized the utf8mb4\_0900\_ai\_ci collation, which was unrecognized by the MariaDB instance in our local XAMPP setup.

As the lead for this phase, I performed a manual "Sanitization" of the SQL script, refactoring the collation to the more compatible utf8mb4\_general\_ci standard. I then moved beyond automated imports to manually provision the users and sellers tables. This manual execution allowed me to verify the structural integrity of the database, ensuring that Foreign Key constraints and Unique Indexes (specifically for email and shopName) were strictly enforced. This meticulous setup was necessary to support the complex relational logic required by the backend creators.

**Phase III: Functional API Validation and Logic Handshake**  
The third phase of my testing cycle involved End-to-End Functional Validation using both Python terminal clients and web-based JavaScript interfaces. During initial connectivity tests with client1.py, I encountered a persistent "Server error or cannot connect" message. To diagnose this, I pivoted from application testing to a manual Network Trace via the browser, which revealed a 404 Not Found error. This diagnostic step allowed me to identify that the API endpoint pathing was misaligned with the local directory structure.

By correcting the routing to http://localhost/ADET/src/service.php, I successfully unlocked the Authentication Handshake. I verified the security logic by confirming that the backend correctly utilized password\_verify() to compare incoming strings against the bcrypt hashes stored in the users table. Furthermore, I performed Regression Testing on the Seller module, validating that the "Register Shop" (POST) and "Update Shop" (PUT) operations were functioning as intended.

**Phase IV: Cross-Platform Interoperability and Final Assessment**  
The final testing milestone involved Cross-Platform Interoperability. I validated the backend's capabilities as a decoupled Web Service by successfully performing the same registration and login lifecycle through client2.html. This confirmed that our CORS (Cross-Origin Resource Sharing) headers were correctly configured to handle requests from both terminal and browser-based frontends.

In conclusion, my contribution to the Artistryx project went beyond simple bug detection. By systematically resolving infrastructure conflicts, refactoring database schemas, and correcting API routing, I ensured that the project moved from a broken state to a fully integrated, stable e-commerce platform. My work as a Tester provided the necessary verification that the system is not only functional but also secure and ready for professional deployment.

# orillana

**Name:** Angel A. Orillana  
**Role:** Client 1 Creator and Backend Contributor (Email and Password validation)

I am responsible for creating one of the required clients for this activity, specifically client1.py. I created it using Python and implemented a menu system. When the program runs, users are greeted with a welcome message and a main menu with three options: 1\. Register, 2\. Login, and 3\. Exit.  
If the user chooses Register, they are asked to input a username, email, and password. If the username or email is already taken, the API returns a message and the user is prompted to try again. Whatever the input, the client will always return a message, whether to inform that the input is incorrect or confirm that the action is successful. The same behavior applies to Login. After a successful login, a welcome message is displayed, followed by a user menu with four options: 1\. Register Shop, 2\. View Shop, 3\. Update Shop, and 4\. Logout.  
In Register Shop, the user is only allowed to register one shop. If they already have one, the system will return a message and prevent them from creating another. It also checks the response to confirm if the shop name is already taken to prevent duplication. For View Shop, the system retrieves the shop details from the API. If the user already has a shop, it will display the shop information such as username, shop name, and shop status. If no shop exists yet, a message will indicate that no shop is found. For Update Shop, the user is allowed to change their existing shop name. Before updating, the system checks the current shop details. If the user inputs a new shop name that already exists or is the same as the current one, the response message will inform the user of the issue. If the update is successful, the updated information will be returned and displayed in the client. For Logout, the user will be redirected back to the main menu. If the user chooses Exit from the main menu, the system will close.  
While developing the client, I encountered challenges in handling API responses and making sure the program guided users properly. At first, some responses were not displayed clearly, which made the flow confusing. For instance, the username and email error message is merged into one, which makes it hard for me to know which of my input is incorrect. I solved this by carefully checking the status codes and adjusting the service.php and how the client printed messages. Another challenge was designing the menu system so that users could navigate smoothly between Register, Login, and the shop functions without getting stuck. I tested different inputs and repeated runs to ensure the client handled invalid choices and errors gracefully.   
With that, I also added restrictions on the email domain, allowing only @gmail.com, @bicol-u.edu.ph, and @outlook.com. For the password, I implemented a rule requiring a minimum of 8 characters, which can be a combination of letters, special characters, and digits, but must not be purely letters, numbers, or symbols. Overall, all messages displayed in the client are based on responses from the API, depending on the status codes returned. This ensures that validation and system rules are handled consistently on the backend, while the client focuses on displaying and handling user interaction.  
 

# serra

**Zsanleigh Peter F. Serra \- BSIT3/C \- AppDev \- Apr 17, 2026**  
**Role \-**   
I was responsible for building Client 2, the 2nd client application that connects to and interacts with our group's PHP REST API (service.php). While another member handled the Python CLI client, my task was to develop a UI-based client, which I first built as a Node.js CLI application and later remade into just a HTML \+ JavaScript browser-based UI to serve as a more accessible mock client for demonstration  
**Task \-**   
1\. Node.js CLI Client (client1.js)  
I built the initial client as a terminal application using Node.js. It presented a numbered menu to the user and accepted input through the command line using the readline module. The client supported all five core API operations:  
Register a new user  
Login (with basic session tracking)  
Create a seller shop  
Read seller information  
Update a shop name  
2\. HTML \+ JavaScript UI Client (client2.html)  
 After the CLI was complete, I remade the client into a simple browser-based UI. Instead of a numbered menu, the user selects an action from a dropdown, which shows only the relevant form, keeping the interface slim and focused. All requests are sent using the browser's native fetch API.  
**Code \-**  
*Request Wrapper (sendRequest)*  
Rather than writing duplicate fetch logic in every function, I did a shared sendRequest(method, url, data) helper. It handles attaching the correct HTTP method, setting Content-Type: application/json, and conditionally adding a request body (skipped for GET requests where params go in the URL instead).  
A design I made also here was the error handling inside the function, since the PHP API sometimes returns plain text or HTML on errors instead of JSON. To handle this, I always read the response as text first, then attempt JSON.parse(). If that fails, I return a structured object with the raw text, so the UI can still display something useful instead of crashing.   
*Session State (globalUserId)*  
After a successful login, I store the returned user ID in a globalUserId variable. This allows the seller-related forms to auto-fill the user ID without requiring the user to type it again every time. If they enter an ID manually, that takes priority over the stored one.  
*Section Toggling (showSection)*  
The HTML client uses a showSection(name) function tied to the dropdown's onchange event. It iterates through all section IDs and sets display: none on all except the selected one. The output area is also cleared on each switch to avoid showing stale responses from a previous action.  
*Error Handling*  
Each action function (doRegister, doLogin, etc.) wraps its request in a try/catch. If the fetch itself throws, like when XAMPP is not running, the catch block calls showError() which displays the error message in the output area instead of silently failing or throwing an unhandled exception.  
**Challenges** \-  
During basic flow testing, some server-side validation errors returned plain text instead of a proper JSON response. This caused JSON.parse() to throw and break the client. I resolved this by parsing the raw response text first and wrapping the parse attempt in its own try/catch.  
the API expects the userId as a query string parameter (?userId=...) rather than a JSON body. This is different from the other POST/PUT operations. I had to handle this as a special case by building the URL string manually and using encodeURIComponent() to ensure the value is URL-safe.  
The first version of the HTML client had no try/catch around the fetch calls in each action function. If the server was unreachable, the app would throw an uncaught promise rejection. I caught this and wrapped every action in proper error handling.  
Since the requirement was a simple mock UI, I deliberately avoided CSS and kept everything in plain HTML. The main challenge was making the interface usable without it looking too cluttered. I solved this by hid	ing all sections by default and only revealing the one matching the dropdown selection.  
**Contribution** \-  
I provided comments to sections that were not immediately readable, like maybe the fallback to globalUserId and the URL encoding for the GET request, to help other group members and myself understand the logic during code review.
